title: Fine-Tuning Basics
lesson:
Fine-tuning means taking a model that someone else already trained and nudging its internal settings so it gets better at your specific job. You are not building a brain from scratch — that part is done. You are sending an already-educated graduate on a short, focused course. This lesson is the map of the whole territory: what fine-tuning actually changes, the main flavours of it, and when you should reach for it instead of a simpler tool.

The hired-chef analogy

Imagine you hire a chef who trained for years and can cook almost any cuisine. That long, expensive education is pretraining — the model has already read most of the internet and learned grammar, facts, and reasoning. But your restaurant only serves one thing: precise, beautiful sushi, plated your house way. You do not re-teach the chef to cook. You give them a few hundred photos of exactly the plate you want, and they adjust. That adjustment is fine-tuning. The chef keeps everything they already knew and layers a new specialty on top.

What is actually changing inside

A model is a giant pile of numbers called parameters — the dials, often billions of them, that decide what word comes next. Fine-tuning shows the model example after example and, each time it answers wrong, slightly tweaks those dials toward the right answer. The size of each tweak is set by the learning rate (how big a step you take when correcting; too big and the model forgets everything, too small and it barely moves). Doing this with a curated set of prompt-and-answer pairs is the most common kind, called SFT (Supervised Fine-Tuning). "Supervised" just means you supply the correct answer each time.

Full fine-tuning vs the cheap, clever way

There are two broad routes. Full fine-tuning tweaks every dial in the model. It is powerful but brutal: a mid-size model can need tens of gigabytes of GPU memory and serious money to update billions of parameters. The smarter modern default is PEFT — Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning. Instead of moving all the dials, you freeze the original model and bolt on a tiny set of new, trainable dials. The most popular form is LoRA, and its memory-saving cousin QLoRA. See the whole family under PEFT.

Second analogy: full fine-tuning is repainting an entire house. PEFT is sticking up a few well-placed posters — far cheaper, fast to swap out, and you can peel them off without damaging the walls. Because a LoRA result is a small separate file (often just tens of megabytes), you can keep one base model and ship dozens of lightweight specialists on top of it.

Full fine-tuningPEFT (e.g. LoRA)
Updates every parameterFreezes the model, trains a tiny add-on
Needs lots of GPU memory + costRuns on a single modest GPU
One big new model fileSmall adapter file, easy to swap
Best for deep behaviour changesGreat for style, tone, niche tasks

A minimal training loop

You rarely write this by hand — libraries do it — but seeing the shape demystifies it. The model reads a batch of examples, measures how wrong it was (the loss, a single number where lower is better), and nudges its dials to lower that number:

for batch in dataset:           # a few examples at a time
    outputs = model(batch)      # model guesses the answers
    loss = outputs.loss         # how wrong it was (lower = better)
    loss.backward()             # work out which dials to nudge
    optimizer.step()            # nudge them by the learning rate
    optimizer.zero_grad()       # reset for the next batch

Run that over your dataset a small number of times (each full pass is an epoch) and the saved result is a checkpoint — a snapshot of the dials you can load and run.

When should you fine-tune at all?

This is the most important judgement call, and beginners get it backwards. Fine-tuning is excellent for teaching behaviour: a consistent format, a brand voice, a narrow skill like "turn this lab report into structured JSON." It is a poor and leaky way to inject fresh facts. If you need the model to know your latest docs or prices, use RAG (feeding facts in at question time) instead of baking them into the dials. A good rule: try a clearer prompt first, then RAG, and only fine-tune when you need a behaviour that prompting cannot reliably produce. Fine-tuning also has richer relatives — Instruction Tuning for following commands, DPO for aligning to human preferences, and Continued Pretraining for soaking up a whole new domain.

⚠️ Fine-tuning can cause catastrophic forgetting: push too hard on a narrow task and the model gets worse at everything else. Keep the learning rate low and the runs short.
mentalModel: Fine-tuning is sending an already-educated graduate on a short, focused course — you keep everything they know and add one specialty. With PEFT you do not even rewire their brain; you hand them a small cheat-sheet (the adapter) they can pick up, swap, or put down.
mistakes: Fine-tuning to add facts. The model stores new facts in a leaky, unreliable way and will still hallucinate. For knowledge that changes or must be exact, use RAG; fine-tune for behaviour and format instead.
Reaching for it too early. Many problems vanish with a sharper prompt or a few in-context examples. Try prompt engineering and RAG first — they are faster, cheaper, and easier to change.

Learning rate too high. Big correction steps make the model overwrite its general skills — "catastrophic forgetting." Start small (a tiny fraction like 1e-4 or 2e-4 for LoRA) and watch that it improves on your task without breaking on others.

Training too long. Run for too many epochs and the model memorises your exact examples (overfitting) instead of learning the pattern. It then nails the training set and flops on anything new. Stop when validation loss stops dropping.
Choosing full fine-tuning by default. It burns memory and money for little gain on most tasks. LoRA / QLoRA usually match it for a fraction of the cost and give you swappable adapter files.

No held-out test set. If you judge only on data the model trained on, great scores mean nothing — it may have just memorised. Always keep a separate slice it never sees during training to measure real progress.
exercise:
Plan (don't run) a fine-tune on paper. Pick one narrow behaviour you want — say, "rewrite any customer email into a polite two-sentence reply." Write down: (1) the exact behaviour in one sentence, (2) why a plain prompt or RAG can't already do it well, (3) whether you'd use full fine-tuning or LoRA and why, and (4) how you'd split your examples into a training set and a held-out test set. Then hand-write 8 prompt-and-ideal-answer pairs in the SFT message format, keeping the answer style identical across all 8.

Stretch goal: Decide your learning rate and number of epochs before training, and write one sentence predicting what would go wrong if you doubled each. (Hint: one risk is forgetting, the other is overfitting.)
solution: Hint: A solid plan looks like — Behaviour: "polite two-sentence email reply." Why not prompt/RAG: prompting gives inconsistent length and tone across edge cases; RAG adds no facts here, so it's irrelevant. Method: LoRA, because the task is pure style/format, needs little memory, and the adapter is easy to swap. Split: e.g. 80% train, 20% held-out test, with no overlap. For the stretch goal: doubling the learning rate risks catastrophic forgetting — the model gets erratic and loses general skill; doubling the epochs risks overfitting — it memorises your 8 examples and reproduces them word-for-word instead of generalising. Good starting points for LoRA are a learning rate around 2e-4 and 1–3 epochs, then stop as soon as the held-out loss stops improving.
summary: Fine-tuning takes an already-trained model and nudges its parameters to specialise it for your task, usually with prompt-and-answer pairs (SFT). Full fine-tuning updates every dial; the cheaper, swappable default is PEFT/LoRA, which freezes the model and trains a tiny add-on. Use it for behaviour, tone, and format — not for storing fresh facts (use RAG for that) — and guard against forgetting and overfitting with a low learning rate, short runs, and a held-out test set.

keyTerms.def: Taking a pretrained model and further training it on a smaller, focused dataset so it gets better at a specific task or style.
The billions of internal numbers (the "dials") that a model adjusts during training to decide what to output.
How big a step the model takes when correcting itself. Too high overwrites old skills; too low barely learns.
A single number measuring how wrong the model's answers are on a batch. Training works to push it down.
One full pass through the entire training dataset. More epochs means more practice, but too many causes overfitting.
Updating every parameter in the model. Powerful but memory-hungry and expensive.
Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning: freeze the original model and train only a small add-on, saving memory and cost.
The most popular PEFT method: it trains tiny extra matrices and saves them as a small, swappable adapter file.
When a model memorises its training examples instead of learning the pattern, so it fails on new inputs.
When fine-tuning too hard on a narrow task makes the model worse at the general skills it used to have.
